summary: The primary factor contributing to the strength of collagen is not solely glycine but also includes essential roles played by hydroxyproline which aids in correct protein folding; deficiencies affecting hydroxyproline result in conditions such as fragile bone disorders indicating its importance beyond mere structural support from amino acids like glycine. Hydroxyproline enables stable formation of a strong three-helix bundle that resists mechanical stress better compared to structures stabilized primarily through other means including simple covalent bonds between adjacent residues formed predominantly with glycine units. To summarize succinctly while focusing strictly on clinically pertinent information regarding what makes up resilient connective tissue proteins specifically related here about their tensile properties – **hydroxyproline** plays an indispensable part alongside glycine ensuring adequate stability under load-bearing demands observed physiologically across various tissues relying heavily upon these extracellular matrix elements for integrity maintenance during daily activities or pathological states alike when compromised leading towards debilitating outcomes indicative thereof e.g., skeletal fragility syndromes noted previously linked back causally attributed mainly toward aberrant processing pathways involving pro-collagen precursors lacking sufficient amounts/quality levels required at each stage transitioning into mature functional forms capable sustaining normal biomechanical functions expected ideally without fail throughout lifespan spanned typically barring any genetic predispositions manifesting abnormally early onset periods potentially disrupting homeostasis prematurely thereby necessitating timely intervention strategies aimed mitigating adverse effects arising secondary complications stemming ultimately from intrinsic flaws present inherently rendering affected individuals more susceptible vulnerabilities otherwise avoidable given appropriate
